title: - WHAT TO PRUNE AND WHAT NOT TO PRUNE AT INI TIALIZATION [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - WHAT TO PRUNE AND WHAT NOT TO PRUNE AT INI TIALIZATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Post-training dropout based approaches achieve high sparsity and are well established means of deciphering problems relating to computational cost and overfitting in Neural Network architectures citesrivastava2014dropout, (Pan et al., 2016), Zhu & Gupta (2017), LeCun et al. (1990). Contrastingly, pruning at initialization is still far behind Frankle et al. (2020). Initialization pruning is more efficacious when it comes to scaling computation cost of the network. Furthermore, it handles overfitting just as well as post training dropout. It is also averse to retraining losses.

In approbation of the above reasons, the paper presents two approaches to prune at initialization. The goal is to achieve higher sparsity while preserving performance. 1) K-starts, begins with k random p-sparse matrices at initialization. In the first couple of epochs the network then determines the ”fittest” of these p-sparse matrices in an attempt to find the ”lottery ticket” Frankle & Carbin (2018) p-sparse network. The approach is adopted from how evolutionary algorithms find the best individual. Depending on the Neural Network architecture, fitness criteria can be based on magnitude of network weights, magnitude of gradient accumulation over an epoch or a combination of both. 2) Dissipating gradients approach, aims at eliminating weights that remain within a fraction of their initial value during the first couple of epochs. Removing weights in this manner despite their magnitude best preserves performance of the network. Contrarily, the approach also takes the most epochs to achieve higher sparsity. 3) Combination of dissipating gradients and kstarts outperforms either methods and random dropout consistently.

The benefits of using the provided pertaining approaches are: 1) They do not require specific knowledge of the classification task, fixing of dropout threshold or regularization parameters 2) Retraining of the model is neither necessary nor affects the performance of the p-sparse network.
We evaluate the efficacy of the said methods on Autoencoders and Fully Connected Multilayered Perceptrons. The datasets used are MNIST and Fashion MNIST.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Computational complexity and overfitting in neural networks is a well established problem Frankle & Carbin (2018), Han et al. (2015), LeCun et al. (1990), Denil et al. (2013). We utilize pruning approaches for the following two reasons: 1) To reduce the computational cost of a fully connected neural network. 2) To reduce overfitting in the network.

Given a large number of post-training pruning approaches Srivastava et al. (2014), Geman et al. (1992), Pan et al. (2016), the paper attempts to propose two pre-training pruning approaches: kstarts and dissipating gradients. Moreover, it appears to be the case that when isolated from other factors sparse networks outperform fully connected networks. When not isolated they perform at least as well up to a percentage of sparsity depending on the number of parameters in the said network. kstarts and dissipating gradients provide are simple nevertheless effective methods to quickly look for best sparse networks to.
The approaches exploit the knowledge that a network has multiple underlying p-sparse networks that perform just as well and in some cases even better when contrasted with their fully connected

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

counterparts Frankle & Carbin (2018). What percentage of sparsity is realized, depends largely on the number of parameters originally present in the network. Such sparse networks are potent in preventing over-fitting and reducing computational cost.

The poset-training pruning has several approaches in place such as adding various regularization schemes to prune the network Louizos et al. (2017), Pan et al. (2016) or using second derivative or hessian of the weights for dropout LeCun et al. (1990), Hassibi & Stork (1993). Han et al. (2015), Alford et al. (2019), Zhu & Gupta (2017) use an efficient iterative pruning method to iteratively increase sparsity. Srivastava et al. (2014) dropout random hidden units with p probability instead of weights to avoid overfitting in general. Each of these approaches is effective and achieves good sparsity post-training.

We use a simple intuitive models that achieve good results and exploits the fact that a number of sub networks in a Neural Network has the potential to individually learn the input Srivastava et al. (2014). We decide on a sparse network early on based on the dropout method and use only that for training. This provides an edge for faster computation, quicker elimination of excess weights and reduced generalization error. The sparsity achieved is superior to random dropout.

Section II gives a general introduction to all the methods, section III defines p-sparsity, section IV provides the algorithm for both approaches, section V describes experimental setup and results, section VI discusses various design choices, section VII gives a general discussion of results, section VIII discusses limitations of the approach and section IX provides conclusions and final remarks.

## 2 PRUNING METHODS

## 2.1 KSTARTS

## 2.1.1 KSTARTS AND EVOLUTIONARY ALGORITHMS

We take the concept of k random starts from Evolutionary Algorithms (Vikhar, 2016) that use a fitness function or heuristic to perform ”natural selection” in optimization and search based problems (Goldberg & Holland, 1988). It is relatively simple to fit genetic algorithms to the problem at hand. Other method that would be equally effective with a little bit of modification are Hunting Search (Oftadeh et al., 2010), Natural Adaptation Strategies (Wierstra et al., 2008), firefly algorithm (Yang, 2010) etc.

The basic components of the algorithm are: (1) **Population** : A product of network weights and sparse matrices. (2) **Individual** : An instance of the population. (3) **Fitness Function** : The heuristic chosen for evaluation of the population.

## 2.1.2 POPULATION

We first initialize K sparse matrices, a single instance of these K sparse matrices can be seen in equation **??** . In every iteration we multiply model weights W of the Network layer in question with every instance of the K sparse matrices. The resulting set of matrices is our population for that iteration. Each iteration is referred to as a new generation.

_population_ = _W ∗ K − SparseMatrices_

(1)

## 2.1.3 INDIVIDUAL

Each individual, in a population of K instances, is a sparse matrix of size equal to the size of network weights, W. The number of 0’s and 1’s in the sparsity matrix are determined by the connectivity factor p which is further described in section 3. An sparse matrix of _p ≈_ 0 _._ 5 will have _≈_ 50% 0’s and _≈_ 50% 1s.

## 2.1.4 EVALUATION/FITNESS FUNCTION

The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1 such that the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 2.

where _i ∗ c_ is the size of each individual and ind refers to the individual in population.

## 2.1.5 NEXT GENERATION SELECTION
Assuming each iteration is the next generation. In each generation the fit individual is favoured so:

- The fittest individual is passed of as weight to the next generation.

- Every 5 generations or as per the decided elimination frequency, the individual with lowest fitness is discarded from the population.

## 2.2 DISSIPATING GRADIENTS

Magnitude and gradient based pruning approaches are popular in post-training pruning Srivastava et al. (2014), LeCun et al. (1990). It does not make much sense to employ them pre-training becauseof the randomly generated weights. But in order to reduce error, any network aims to target updating weights that influence the results most. Based upon that hypothesis, in each epoch we sum gradients over and eliminate weights whose weights are not getting updated. In equation 3 N is the total number of iterations for an epoch. In equation 4 epsilon is 1e-6 for all experiments.

One consideration in this approach is to not do this for too many epochs which can be only 2 if the image is very monochrome and more than 2 if the gradients are dissipating more slowly. Moreover, once specific weights have reached their optimal learning, their gradients will dissipate and we don’t want to eliminate them.

## 2.3 COMBINATION DROPOUT

Combination dropout is merely combining Kstarts with dissipating gradients. The weights eliminated use both approaches. We fix p for Kstarts to a certain value of minimum sparsity and further eliminate weights that dissipating gradients method will eliminate as well. The approach achieves better performance than either methods.

## 3 DEFINING P-SPARSITY

In a p-sparse network layer approximately p percent of connection between two layers are eliminated. Figure 1 shows a fully connected conventional neural network (figure 1a) and three sparsely connected networks with different values of p (figures 1b, 1c, 1d).

## 3.1 CONNECTIVITY FACTOR

Connectivity factor, p, determines the percentage of connections to be removed between any two layers of the network. For instance if p = 0.0 than the network is be fully connected as shown in figure 1a, if on the opposite extreme, p=1.0, then there will be no connections between two layers of neurons. If p=0.5, figure 1b, only approximately 50% of the connection in the network remain. If p=0.3, figure 1c, approximately 70% of the connection still exist and if p=0.7, figure 1d, a mere 30% of the connections are active between two network layers. In short p determines percentage of 0’s in an individual sparse matrix shown in equation **??** .

## 4 ALGORITHM

The autoencoder, two and three layered neural networks are trained in a standard manner with adam optimizer and batch update.

## 4.1 KSTARTS ALGORITHM

Algorithm 1 gives the kstarts method:

1. We have K number of sparse matrices as shown in equation **??** generated and we call them KI in algorithm 1.

2. Every time the weight W needs to be updated instead of the usual gradient update we add one step further and using the fitness function, pass the fittest individual as the new W which biases the network towards that sparse matrix.

3. Every 5 iterations, the individual with lowest fitness is dropped.

**Algorithm 1:** k Random starts

**input :** Data, params,K,p **output:** W,b initialize W,b; _KI ←_ K individuals with approximately 1-p percent active connections; **for** _maxiterations_ **do** Run Neural Network; Update weights; **if** _One individual in KI is left_ **then** W _←_ individual; **else** W _←_ individual with maximum sum (of weights); **for** _every 5 iterations_ **do** pop individual with minimum sum (of weights) from KI; **end end end**

- 4.2 DISSIPATING GRADIENTS ALGORITHM

Algorithm 2 is more simple and just eliminates weights with sum of gradients equal to zero in the first 1-4 epochs depending on the desired sparsity.

Figure 2: Performance of dropout methods with increasing sparsity.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS AND RESULTS

The experiments performed on two datasets; MNIST Deng (2012) and Fashion MNIST Xiao et al. (2017). The network architectures are two layered Autoencoder (784-128-64), a three layered NN (784-128-100-10) both with sigmoid activation and adam optimization functions.

The Architecture used for learning curves is a single layered NN(784-10).

5

**----- Start of picture text -----**

(a) Dataset : Fashion MNIST (b) Dataset : MNIST
**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 3: Effect of sample size on learning when varying the sparsity of the network with kstarts. Each trial involves running a p-sparse network on a fixed training set of N examples from dataset for 2.5k iterations and is averaged over 10 trials each.

## 5.1 EFFECT OF INCREASING SPARSITY

As sparsity increases, overall performance reduces. Figure 2 shows the behaviour of various dropout methods presented in this paper.

In case of random dropout, it’s indeed a random shot. Either no useful weight is eliminated or multiple crucial weights are eliminated which decides how well does random dropout perform. Kstarts performs slightly better on average with multiple start choices. Depending on how many independent p-sparse networks are present in the network that can learn well, one of them can be identified, given k is large enough and the fitness function is smartly decided by first examining the weights and gradients.

Dissipating gradients works well as long as the network isn’t learning very fast i.e. some weights are being updated in the consequent epochs. It’s also most reliable. Combination works by far the best because it does not only rely on eliminating weights that are not being updated but also uses kstarts. It seems to achieve superior performance as long as p value chosen is a value that kstarts performs well on.

## 5.2 VARIATION IN SAMPLE SIZE

Figures 3a and 3b show relationship of varying sample size to different sparsity values in a single layer NN over 2.5k iterations.

The interesting result here is that isolated from all other factors like number of parameters, hidden units and various design choices, kstarts dropout performs better on a single layer network compared to even a fully connected network. The standard deviation is also lower to fully connected network as well as random dropout. kstarts dropout also learns faster than a fully connected network. For instance, if the iterations for the experiments in figure 3a and 3b are increased, the fully connected network will eventually reach accuracy of the p-sparse network.

## 6 DESIGN PARAMETER CHOICES

There are a number of design parameter choices for the algorithm 1 presented here. Some are explained in detail in this section.

## 6.1 CHOICE OF FITNESS FUNCTION

Since the fitness function determines the individual being passed on to the next generation and the individual being eliminated. We had three choices for choosing the fitness of an individual each with it’s own pros and cons.

6

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

- **Magnitude:** As opted here, we choose population as shown in equation 1 and then select fitness using equation 2. This skews the selection of new weights to the previously selected sparse matrix from KI and therefore, the initial sparse matrix will be propagated forward. This also renders elimination to be pointless it does not matter if 1 or all other matrices in K are eliminated. Furthermore, the sparse matrix is picked awfully early in the experiments i.e. only after first 5 iterations or so and that is not when weights have reached a saturation point.
- **Gradient:** The second choice is to use gradient of the weights to create population as follows:

- _population_ = _δW ∗ SparseMatrices_ (5)

- doing so can have fitness totally dependant on the current update and the new weights are heavily skewed towards the performance of the current iterations which again doesn’t seem that appropriate.
- **Sum of Gradients:** The third option is summing up the gradients for a number of iterations in the manner we do for dissipating gradients 3 and then use those to create population:

Doing so skews the network toward weights that are updated quickly and are increasing.

## 6.2 NO. OF LAYERS AND NO. HIDDEN UNITS

We initially use single layer NN (784-10) to isolate the effects of kstarts algorithm from effects of other parameters that may have a large impact on performance as the size of the network grows. Those parameters i.e. number of hidden layers, regularization choices, types of layers may aid or adversely effect the performance of the algorithm and by performing the experiments on the basic unit of a neural network we were able to concur that the method is effective in it’s own right. After concluding the approach works we tested on three layered NN and Autoencoder.

## 6.3 COST EFFECTIVESNESS

One beneficial feature of pre-training pruning approaches is that the best p-sparse network is quickly identified. There are a number of methods that exploit sparsity of a matrix for quicker multiplication i.e. (Yuster & Zwick, 2005), (Buluc¸ & Gilbert, 2012) which can be used to quickly retrain large networks. Although that is out of scope for our findings.

## 7 DISCUSSION

- 7.1 EFFECT OF K

1. From the experiments done so far lower K ( _≈_ 10) outperforms or at least performs as well as a higher value of K ( _≈_ 100). This can be because the more times a different matrix is chosen by the network, the more times the network has to adopt to learning with only that p-sparse network.

## 7.2 EFFECT OF P

1. A sparse network can outperforms a fully connected network for lower number of iterations and smaller networks.

2. An appropriate value of p, for instance in these experiments _p ≈_ 0 _._ 5, seems to work best for random dropout, kstarts dropout and combination dropout. A poor choice of p can’t seem to be remedied by a better choice of K.

3. p can be thought of as an information limiter. For better learning, if the network is only provided with particular features, it might have an easier time learning the class specific features in different nodes but this only remains a wishful speculation and requires further analysis. Table 1 shows relationship between k,p and no of iterations in a single layer NN (784-10)

Table 1: MNIST-Mean Accuracy and standard deviation averaged over 10 runs

## 8 LIMITATIONS

There are a number of limitations to the approach and further investigation is required in a number of domains.
1. The NNs used are single and three layered feed forward networks and autoencoders. CNNs are not experimented upon.
2. Only classification tasks are considered and those on only two datasets: MNIST and Fashion MNIST.
3. Ideally using sparse matrix should make for efficient computation but since the algorithms for that are not used it at this point does not show what the time comparison of the approaches will be.

## 9 CONCLUSIONS AND FINAL REMARKS

We present two methods for pruning weights pre-training or in the first couple of epochs. The comparisons are made against random dropout and both approaches mostly perform better than the random dropout. We provide a combination dropout approach that consistently outperforms other dropout approaches. A lot more analysis of the approach is required on multiple datasets, learning tasks and network architectures but the basic methods seem to be effective.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper proposes two initialization-pruning methods (K-starts and dissipating gradients) for neural networks, aiming to achieve sparsity without retraining. While the topic is timely and relevant to ongoing discussions around the Lottery Ticket Hypothesis and pruning-at-initialization (e.g., SNIP, GraSP, SynFlow), the submission suffers from serious issues that would likely preclude acceptance at a venue like ICLR. **Writing and clarity** are major concerns: the abstract and introduction contain numerous grammatical errors, malformed sentences ("kstarts and dissipating gradients provide are simple nevertheless effective methods to quickly look for best sparse networks to."), a broken citation ("citesrivastava2014dropout"), an unresolved LaTeX reference ("equation ??"), and typos ("poset-training," "approbation"). The structure is also confused—the abstract enumerates "two approaches" but then lists three items. **Technical novelty and rigor** appear limited: the K-starts method essentially amounts to selecting the random mask whose element-wise product with initial weights has the largest magnitude sum, which is a weak heuristic with little theoretical motivation and closely resembles simple magnitude-based masking; the evolutionary-algorithm framing feels superficial rather than substantive. **Experimental scope** is narrow—only MNIST and Fashion-MNIST on MLPs and autoencoders—which is insufficient to support claims of general efficacy, especially given that the state-of-the-art baselines (SNIP, GraSP, SynFlow, Lottery Ticket) are not mentioned as comparison points in what is shown. **Claims are overstated and unsupported**, particularly the assertions that the method handles overfitting "just as well as post-training dropout" and outperforms alternatives "consistently," without the empirical evidence visible in the introduction to back them up. Finally, the related-work discussion conflates weight pruning with dropout and misrepresents the pruning-at-initialization literature. A substantial rewrite, stronger baselines, broader experiments, and clearer technical justification would be needed.

comments.title: Population equation notation is ambiguous/incorrect
comments.quote: _population_ = _W ∗ K − SparseMatrices_
comments.explanation: Reading this as written, 'W * K - SparseMatrices' looks like a subtraction (W times K, minus SparseMatrices). From the surrounding prose ('we multiply model weights W of the Network layer in question with every instance of the K sparse matrices'), the intended meaning is the elementwise product of W with each of the K sparse matrices, i.e., population = {W ⊙ S_i : i=1..K}. The hyphen between K and SparseMatrices appears to be meant as a descriptor ('K-SparseMatrices'), not subtraction, but as a formal equation it is confusingly written and does not convey a set of K matrices. This is a moderate notation problem because the population object is central to the algorithm.

comments.title: Fitness function 'equation 2' not shown; ranking criterion underspecified
comments.quote: The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1 such that the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 2.
comments.explanation: The passage references equation 2 for the fittest individual but no equation 2 appears in the extracted text — only equation (1), the population definition. The text says fitness is 'the sum of each individual,' and later in Section 6.1 it is clarified as 'sum (of weights)' of W*S_i. However, within Section 2.1.4 itself, the reader is told the key formula is 'given by equation 2,' which is missing. Combined with the dangling phrase 'where i*c is the size of each individual,' the fitness function is underspecified at the point it is introduced. A reader cannot reproduce the exact fitness criterion from this section alone.

comments.title: Broken reference to sparse matrix equation
comments.quote: We first initialize K sparse matrices, a single instance of these K sparse matrices can be seen in equation **??** .
comments.explanation: The equation reference is unresolved ('equation ??'), and the same broken reference occurs again in Section 3.1 ('an individual sparse matrix shown in equation ??'). Since no explicit equation defining the form of an individual sparse matrix appears in the shown text, readers are left without a formal definition of the sparse-matrix object — only the prose that it has p fraction of zeros. This is a real gap (not just typesetting) because the individual is a core object of the algorithm.

comments.title: Claim that initialization pruning handles overfitting 'just as well' as post-training dropout is unsupported
comments.quote: Furthermore, it handles overfitting just as well as post training dropout.
comments.explanation: This is stated as an established fact in the abstract's motivation. The paper's own experiments (MNIST / Fashion-MNIST, small MLPs and autoencoders) are not sufficient to back this general claim, and the cited Frankle et al. (2020) paper ('Why are we missing the mark?') actually argues that initialization pruning is behind post-training pruning. The authors themselves write that initialization pruning 'is still far behind' in the very next sentence cluster. The overfitting-equivalence claim is therefore an overclaim that is not demonstrated in the paper and sits in tension with the adjacent citation.

comments.title: Threshold inconsistency between text (1e-6) and Algorithm 2 (0.0001)
comments.quote: if  accumulated d W <  0 . 0001  then
comments.explanation: Algorithm 2 uses a threshold of 0.0001 to decide whether to zero out a weight based on accumulated gradients. The surrounding text, however, states that 'epsilon is 1e-6 for all experiments.' These two values differ by two orders of magnitude. This is a substantive contradiction between the prose specification and the algorithmic pseudocode. Either the algorithm or the prose is wrong, and a reader cannot tell which. Additionally, the text refers to equations 3 and 4 which do not appear, making the method further underspecified.

comments.title: Algorithm 2 underspecified: accumulated_dW reset behavior unclear
comments.explanation: After setting accumulated_dW to 0 or 1 and multiplying W by it, the accumulated_dW is not reset to zero at the start of the next outer epoch. This means once a weight survives one epoch, accumulated_dW is set to 1, and in the next epoch the gradient sum starts from 1 rather than 0, which would affect the threshold comparison. The algorithm is underspecified on whether accumulated_dW is reset at the start of each epoch, making reproduction difficult.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 pseudocode nests loops incorrectly
comments.quote: **for** _maxiterations_ **do** Run Neural Network; Update weights; **if** _One individual in KI is left_ **then** W _←_ individual; **else** W _←_ individual with maximum sum (of weights); **for** _every 5 iterations_ **do** pop individual with minimum sum (of weights) from KI; **end end end**
comments.explanation: The pseudocode shows a 'for every 5 iterations' loop nested inside the main maxiterations loop, inside the else branch. As written, this inner loop would run every iteration (not every 5 iterations), and it's nested inside the else branch so it only runs when more than one individual remains. The intent stated in the text is 'Every 5 iterations, the individual with lowest fitness is dropped.' This should be a conditional check (if iteration % 5 == 0) rather than a nested loop. The pseudocode is misleading as to how the elimination frequency is implemented.

comments.title: Table 1 column header inconsistency and unexplained non-monotonic results
comments.explanation: The table header lists p=0.0 and then what appears to be p=0.5 twice, likely an OCR artifact of a merged cell. More concerning is the data: the iter=100 row shows 50.53 for k=10, 46.19 for k=50, and only 14.83 for k=100 — this non-monotonic behavior (performance collapsing at k=100) is striking and not discussed. A reader trying to verify the claim that 'lower K (~10) outperforms or at least performs as well as a higher value of K (~100)' will find this table supports it dramatically at iter=100 but barely at iter=10k. The table header ambiguity plus the unexplained dramatic drop at k=100, iter=100 warrants clarification.

comments.title: Magnitude fitness critique undermines the method actually used
comments.quote: **Magnitude:** As opted here, we choose population as shown in equation 1 and then select fitness using equation 2. This skews the selection of new weights to the previously selected sparse matrix from KI and therefore, the initial sparse matrix will be propagated forward. This also renders elimination to be pointless it does not matter if 1 or all other matrices in K are eliminated. Furthermore, the sparse matrix is picked awfully early in the experiments i.e. only after first 5 iterations or so and that is not when weights have reached a saturation point.
comments.explanation: This is the authors' self-assessment of the fitness function they actually use ('As opted here'). They candidly acknowledge that magnitude-based selection (a) locks in the initially selected sparse matrix, (b) makes the elimination step pointless, and (c) converges too early before weights saturate. These are serious concerns about the core algorithm (Algorithm 1) used throughout the experiments. Yet the paper never resolves this — the alternative fitness functions are presented only to be critiqued as also inappropriate, and no experiments compare them. A reader is left wondering why the authors stuck with a method they themselves describe as flawed, and whether results in Table 1 and Figure 2 would change substantially with a better fitness function. This undermines the central claim that kstarts 'finds the lottery ticket' — by the authors' own reasoning, it just locks in a random sparse matrix chosen after ~5 iterations.

comments.title: Sum of gradients fitness equation missing
comments.quote:
**Sum of Gradients:** The third option is summing up the gradients for a number of iterations in the manner we do for dissipating gradients 3 and then use those to create population:

Doing so skews the network toward weights that are updated quickly and are increasing.
comments.explanation: The text promises an equation ('create population:') but no equation follows — the next sentence starts with 'Doing so skews...'. Compare to the Gradient variant which does show equation (5). This leaves the Sum of Gradients option underspecified: does it use accumulated dW in place of W, or in place of δW? Over what window? Since the fitness function is a core design choice and the authors explicitly discuss trade-offs among three options, omitting the formal definition of one of them is a real gap.

comments.title: Claim that combination 'consistently outperforms' not fully supported
comments.quote: We provide a combination dropout approach that consistently outperforms other dropout approaches.
comments.explanation: The conclusion states combination dropout 'consistently outperforms' other approaches. The earlier Section 5.1 text says combination 'works by far the best ... as long as p value chosen is a value that kstarts performs well on' — i.e., with a caveat. The conclusion drops this caveat. For a reader relying only on the conclusion, this is a mild overclaim: the paper's own prose establishes that combination's superiority is conditional on p being in kstarts' good-performance range.

progressive_original__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive Original (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive_original__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper proposes two initialization-pruning methods (K-starts and dissipating gradients) for neural networks, aiming to achieve sparsity without retraining. While the topic is timely and relevant to ongoing discussions around the Lottery Ticket Hypothesis and pruning-at-initialization (e.g., SNIP, GraSP, SynFlow), the submission suffers from serious issues that would likely preclude acceptance at a venue like ICLR. **Writing and clarity** are major concerns: the abstract and introduction contain numerous grammatical errors, malformed sentences ("kstarts and dissipating gradients provide are simple nevertheless effective methods to quickly look for best sparse networks to."), a broken citation ("citesrivastava2014dropout"), an unresolved LaTeX reference ("equation ??"), and typos ("poset-training," "approbation"). The structure is also confused—the abstract enumerates "two approaches" but then lists three items. **Technical novelty and rigor** appear limited: the K-starts method essentially amounts to selecting the random mask whose element-wise product with initial weights has the largest magnitude sum, which is a weak heuristic with little theoretical motivation and closely resembles simple magnitude-based masking; the evolutionary-algorithm framing feels superficial rather than substantive. **Experimental scope** is narrow—only MNIST and Fashion-MNIST on MLPs and autoencoders—which is insufficient to support claims of general efficacy, especially given that the state-of-the-art baselines (SNIP, GraSP, SynFlow, Lottery Ticket) are not mentioned as comparison points in what is shown. **Claims are overstated and unsupported**, particularly the assertions that the method handles overfitting "just as well as post-training dropout" and outperforms alternatives "consistently," without the empirical evidence visible in the introduction to back them up. Finally, the related-work discussion conflates weight pruning with dropout and misrepresents the pruning-at-initialization literature. A substantial rewrite, stronger baselines, broader experiments, and clearer technical justification would be needed.

comments.title: Population equation notation is ambiguous/incorrect
comments.quote: _population_ = _W ∗ K − SparseMatrices_
comments.explanation: Reading this as written, 'W * K - SparseMatrices' looks like a subtraction (W times K, minus SparseMatrices). From the surrounding prose ('we multiply model weights W of the Network layer in question with every instance of the K sparse matrices'), the intended meaning is the elementwise product of W with each of the K sparse matrices, i.e., population = {W ⊙ S_i : i=1..K}. The hyphen between K and SparseMatrices appears to be meant as a descriptor ('K-SparseMatrices'), not subtraction, but as a formal equation it is confusingly written and does not convey a set of K matrices. This is a moderate notation problem because the population object is central to the algorithm.

comments.title: Fitness function 'equation 2' not shown; ranking criterion underspecified
comments.quote: The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1 such that the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 2.
comments.explanation: The passage references equation 2 for the fittest individual but no equation 2 appears in the extracted text — only equation (1), the population definition. The text says fitness is 'the sum of each individual,' and later in Section 6.1 it is clarified as 'sum (of weights)' of W*S_i. However, within Section 2.1.4 itself, the reader is told the key formula is 'given by equation 2,' which is missing. Combined with the dangling phrase 'where i*c is the size of each individual,' the fitness function is underspecified at the point it is introduced. A reader cannot reproduce the exact fitness criterion from this section alone.

comments.title: Broken reference to sparse matrix equation
comments.quote: We first initialize K sparse matrices, a single instance of these K sparse matrices can be seen in equation **??** .
comments.explanation: The equation reference is unresolved ('equation ??'), and the same broken reference occurs again in Section 3.1 ('an individual sparse matrix shown in equation ??'). Since no explicit equation defining the form of an individual sparse matrix appears in the shown text, readers are left without a formal definition of the sparse-matrix object — only the prose that it has p fraction of zeros. This is a real gap (not just typesetting) because the individual is a core object of the algorithm.

comments.title: Fitness-based selection may trivially lock onto the initial sparse matrix
comments.quote: The fittest individual is passed of as weight to the next generation.
comments.explanation: Taken together with the fitness definition (sum of W⊙S_i) and the fact that the chosen individual's W is passed forward, the authors themselves later note in Section 6.1 that 'the initial sparse matrix will be propagated forward' and that 'elimination [is] pointless.' This is a self-acknowledged weakness, but the Section 2.1.5 description is presented as the method without any caveat. A reader following only Section 2 would not realize that the 'selection' is essentially deterministic after the first iteration. The method description here overstates the role of evolutionary selection relative to what actually happens.

comments.title: Claim that initialization pruning handles overfitting 'just as well' as post-training dropout is unsupported
comments.quote: Furthermore, it handles overfitting just as well as post training dropout.
comments.explanation: This is stated as an established fact in the abstract's motivation. The paper's own experiments (MNIST / Fashion-MNIST, small MLPs and autoencoders) are not sufficient to back this general claim, and the cited Frankle et al. (2020) paper ('Why are we missing the mark?') actually argues that initialization pruning is behind post-training pruning. The authors themselves write that initialization pruning 'is still far behind' in the very next sentence cluster. The overfitting-equivalence claim is therefore an overclaim that is not demonstrated in the paper and sits in tension with the adjacent citation.

comments.title: Missing equations referenced in text
comments.quote: In equation 3 N is the total number of iterations for an epoch. In equation 4 epsilon is 1e-6 for all experiments.
comments.explanation: The text refers to equation 3 and equation 4 which should define the gradient accumulation and the elimination threshold for the Dissipating Gradients method. However, no such equations appear in the passage — only a prose description follows. Looking at Algorithm 2, the threshold used is actually 0.0001 (1e-4), not 1e-6 as claimed in the text. This is a genuine inconsistency: the text says epsilon is 1e-6 but the pseudocode uses 0.0001. A reader trying to reproduce the method would be confused about which threshold is correct. Additionally, the equations themselves are missing, making the method underspecified.

comments.title: Threshold inconsistency between text and algorithm
comments.quote: if  accumulated d W <  0 . 0001  then
comments.explanation: Algorithm 2 uses a threshold of 0.0001 to decide whether to zero out a weight based on accumulated gradients. The surrounding text, however, states that 'epsilon is 1e-6 for all experiments.' These two values differ by two orders of magnitude. This is not a trivial OCR issue (both are clearly written) but a substantive contradiction between the prose specification and the algorithmic pseudocode. Either the algorithm or the prose is wrong, and a reader cannot tell which without additional information.

comments.title: Algorithm 2 logic likely inverts the intended elimination rule
comments.explanation: Reading carefully: if accumulated dW is below threshold (i.e., weight is NOT being updated), it is set to 0, and then W ← W * 0 eliminates that weight. This matches the stated intent: 'eliminate weights whose weights are not getting updated.' So the logic is actually correct. However, there's still an issue: after setting accumulated_dW to 0 or 1 and multiplying W by it, the accumulated_dW is not reset to zero at the start of the next outer epoch. This means once a weight survives one epoch, accumulated_dW is set to 1, and in the next epoch the gradient sum starts from 1 rather than 0, which would seem to affect the threshold comparison. The algorithm is underspecified on whether accumulated_dW is reset at the start of each epoch (it is initialized implicitly but not explicitly reset inside the maxepochs loop). This makes reproduction difficult.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 pseudocode nests loops incorrectly
comments.quote: **for** _maxiterations_ **do** Run Neural Network; Update weights; **if** _One individual in KI is left_ **then** W _←_ individual; **else** W _←_ individual with maximum sum (of weights); **for** _every 5 iterations_ **do** pop individual with minimum sum (of weights) from KI; **end end end**
comments.explanation: The pseudocode shows a 'for every 5 iterations' loop nested inside the main maxiterations loop, inside the else branch. As written, this inner loop would run every iteration (not every 5 iterations), and it's nested inside the else branch so it only runs when more than one individual remains. The intent stated in the text is 'Every 5 iterations, the individual with lowest fitness is dropped.' This should be a conditional check (if iteration % 5 == 0) rather than a nested loop. The pseudocode is underspecified and misleading as to how the elimination frequency is implemented.

comments.title: Contradiction between K reduction claim and Algorithm 1
comments.quote: **if** _One individual in KI is left_ **then** W _←_ individual;
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 includes a branch handling the case where only one individual remains in KI, implying the population eventually shrinks to one. However, if elimination happens every 5 iterations starting from K individuals, K would shrink to 1 after 5(K-1) iterations. For K=100 (mentioned in Section 7.1), that's 495 iterations — very early in training. This means the 'selection' process is effectively over quickly, and the remainder of training uses a fixed sparse mask. This is a legitimate feature of the method, but the description leaves unclear whether the elimination continues indefinitely, whether K shrinks to 1 during training, or whether the population is replenished. Combined with the nested loop issue above, the algorithm is underspecified.

comments.title: Figure 1 label inconsistency with text
comments.quote: If p=0.5, figure 1b, only approximately 50% of the connection in the network remain. If p=0.3, figure 1c, approximately 70% of the connection still exist and if p=0.7, figure 1d
comments.explanation: The text says figure 1b shows p=0.5, 1c shows p=0.3, and 1d shows p=0.7. But the figure caption text lists them as: (b) 0.5-Sparse, (c) 0.3-Sparse, (d) 0.7-Sparse. This matches. However, normally one would expect figures to be ordered by increasing sparsity (0.3, 0.5, 0.7), not the order given (0.5, 0.3, 0.7). This isn't an error per se, but is a mild ambiguity/awkwardness. Not worth flagging as an actual issue since the labels are consistent between text and caption.

comments.title: Table 1 column header inconsistency
comments.explanation: The table header lists p=0.0 and then what appears to be p=0.5 twice. Looking at the structure — one column under p=0.0 (k=1) and then four columns under p=0.5 (k=1, k=10, k=50, k=100) — the duplicate 'p=0.5' header is likely an OCR artifact of a merged cell spanning columns. However, more concerning is the data in the k=1 p=0.5 column: values like 13.36, 28.96, 63.39, 88.40 are very close to the p=0.0 values (12.29, 28.41, 60.71, 82.49), which makes sense if k=1 at p=0.5 is essentially a single random sparse network. But then the iter=100 row shows 50.53 for k=10, 46.19 for k=50, and only 14.83 for k=100 — this non-monotonic behavior (performance collapsing at k=100) is striking and not discussed. A reader trying to verify the claim that 'lower K (~10) outperforms or at least performs as well as a higher value of K (~100)' will find this table supports it dramatically at iter=100 but barely at iter=10k. The table header ambiguity plus the unexplained dramatic drop at k=100, iter=100 warrants clarification.

comments.title: Magnitude fitness critique undermines the method actually used
comments.quote: **Magnitude:** As opted here, we choose population as shown in equation 1 and then select fitness using equation 2. This skews the selection of new weights to the previously selected sparse matrix from KI and therefore, the initial sparse matrix will be propagated forward. This also renders elimination to be pointless it does not matter if 1 or all other matrices in K are eliminated. Furthermore, the sparse matrix is picked awfully early in the experiments i.e. only after first 5 iterations or so and that is not when weights have reached a saturation point.
comments.explanation: This is the authors' self-assessment of the fitness function they actually use ('As opted here'). They are candidly acknowledging that magnitude-based selection (a) locks in the initially selected sparse matrix, (b) makes the elimination step pointless, and (c) converges too early before weights saturate. These are serious concerns about the core algorithm (Algorithm 1) used throughout the experiments. Yet the paper never resolves this — the alternative fitness functions (Gradient, Sum of Gradients) are presented only to be critiqued as also inappropriate, and no experiments compare them. A reader is left wondering why the authors stuck with a method they themselves describe as flawed, and whether results in Table 1 and Figure 2 would change substantially with a better fitness function. This undermines the central claim that kstarts 'finds the lottery ticket' — by the authors' own reasoning, it just locks in a random sparse matrix chosen after ~5 iterations.

comments.title: Sum of gradients fitness equation missing
comments.quote:
**Sum of Gradients:** The third option is summing up the gradients for a number of iterations in the manner we do for dissipating gradients 3 and then use those to create population:

Doing so skews the network toward weights that are updated quickly and are increasing.
comments.explanation: The text promises an equation ('create population:') but no equation follows — the next sentence starts with 'Doing so skews...'. Compare to the Gradient variant which does show equation (5). This leaves the Sum of Gradients option underspecified: does it use accumulated dW in place of W, or in place of δW? Over what window? Since the fitness function is a core design choice and the authors explicitly discuss trade-offs among three options, omitting the formal definition of one of them is a real gap — particularly since the critique of the magnitude variant suggests a reader might want to try the Sum of Gradients variant.

comments.title: Claim that combination 'consistently outperforms' not fully supported in shown passage
comments.quote: We provide a combination dropout approach that consistently outperforms other dropout approaches.
comments.explanation: The conclusion states combination dropout 'consistently outperforms' other approaches. The earlier Section 5.1 text says combination 'works by far the best ... as long as p value chosen is a value that kstarts performs well on' — i.e., with a caveat. The conclusion drops this caveat. For a reader relying only on the conclusion, this is a mild overclaim: the paper's own prose establishes that combination's superiority is conditional on p being in kstarts' good-performance range. Minor because the qualification appears elsewhere in the paper.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper tackles an interesting and relevant problem—pruning at initialization—but in its current form the quality and clarity are substantially below the standard expected for a top ML conference. The motivation is only loosely connected to the existing literature, with several questionable or unsupported claims, e.g., that initialization pruning “handles overfitting just as well as post-training dropout” or is “averse to retraining losses,” and the distinction between dropout, pruning, sparsity, and lottery-ticket-style subnetworks is often blurred. The proposed methods are not described with sufficient precision: key definitions such as p-sparsity, the exact fitness function, the update/selection procedure, and how gradients are used in “dissipating gradients” are either missing, ambiguous, or mathematically under-specified. The writing contains many grammatical errors, broken citations, formatting artifacts, missing equation references, and awkward phrasing, which makes the technical content difficult to assess. More importantly, the novelty is unclear relative to existing pruning-at-initialization methods such as SNIP, GraSP, SynFlow, and lottery-ticket variants, which should be central baselines and points of comparison. The experimental scope also appears too limited—MNIST/Fashion-MNIST with autoencoders and fully connected MLPs is insufficient to support broad claims about neural network pruning—and the paper will need rigorous baselines, ablations, statistical reporting, computational cost analysis, and clearer definitions of sparsity/performance tradeoffs. Overall, the idea of selecting among multiple sparse masks early in training may be worth exploring, but the current manuscript requires major improvements in technical rigor, positioning, presentation, and empirical validation.

comments.title: Dropout is conflated with pruning/sparsity-producing methods
comments.quote: Post-training dropout based approaches achieve high sparsity and are well established means of deciphering problems relating to computational cost and overfitting in Neural Network architectures citesrivastava2014dropout, (Pan et al., 2016), Zhu & Gupta (2017), LeCun et al. (1990).
comments.explanation: The paper repeatedly appears to use “dropout” loosely for pruning or sparsity-producing methods, while citing both dropout-style regularization and pruning papers together. Standard dropout in the sense of Srivastava et al. (2014) does not by itself produce a sparse final network or reduce inference-time computation; the full dense network is normally used at test time. Pruning methods such as LeCun et al. or Han et al. have different objectives and implementations. Since the paper’s claimed contribution is pruning at initialization, this conflation can mislead readers about the baseline being improved upon and about whether sparsity or computational benefits follow from the cited prior work.

comments.title: Computational-speed and broad efficacy claims are not supported by the experiments
comments.quote: Initialization pruning is more efficacious when it comes to scaling computation cost of the network. Furthermore, it handles overfitting just as well as post training dropout. It is also averse to retraining losses.
comments.explanation: The paper claims advantages in computation scaling, speed, overfitting control, and retraining losses, but the reported experiments are limited to MNIST/Fashion-MNIST with small fully connected networks and autoencoders. The limitations section also states that sparse-matrix implementations and time comparisons are not used. A reduction in active parameters does not necessarily imply wall-clock speedup without appropriate sparse kernels or structured sparsity, and K-starts may even add overhead by evaluating multiple masks. The paper can claim parameter sparsity or accuracy preservation in the tested settings, but the broader computational and overfitting claims require runtime/FLOP evidence, sparse implementation details, or stronger theoretical justification.

comments.title: Dissipating gradients is described as distance from initialization but implemented as accumulated-gradient pruning
comments.quote: 2) Dissipating gradients approach, aims at eliminating weights that remain within a fraction of their initial value during the first couple of epochs.
comments.explanation: The prose describes pruning weights that remain close to their initial values, but the later algorithm appears to accumulate gradients over iterations and zero weights whose accumulated gradient is below a threshold. These are not equivalent criteria. A weight can remain near its initial value because signed gradients cancel, because the learning rate is small, or because it is already useful; conversely, a small accumulated gradient does not directly measure distance from the initial value. The paper’s intuition and reproducibility depend on clearly specifying whether the pruning statistic is displacement from initialization, accumulated gradient, absolute accumulated gradient, or something else.

comments.title: K-starts population, mask application, and “individual” terminology are underspecified
comments.quote: _population_ = _W ∗ K − SparseMatrices_
comments.explanation: The intended operation seems to be applying each of K binary sparsity masks to the current weight matrix, but the notation is ambiguous: it could be read as an ordinary matrix product, an elementwise/Hadamard product, or a set construction. The ambiguity is compounded because an “individual” is sometimes described as a masked weight matrix and elsewhere as a binary sparse matrix of 0s and 1s. These are different objects with different implications for fitness evaluation, gradient updates, elimination, and inheritance. For reproducibility, the paper should explicitly state whether individuals are masks or masked weights, whether masks are binary and per-layer, whether they are sampled once or resampled, and how gradients flow through masked entries.

comments.title: K-starts weight replacement may collapse the population and make elimination/K dynamics unclear
comments.quote: Every time the weight W needs to be updated instead of the usual gradient update we add one step further and using the fitness function, pass the fittest individual as the new W which biases the network towards that sparse matrix.
comments.explanation: The high-level idea of K-starts is to compare several sparse candidate subnetworks, but the algorithm says to replace the actual weight matrix W by the selected masked individual. If W is set to W ⊙ M_j and the next generation again forms W ⊙ M_k, then weights zeroed by the selected mask remain unavailable to all later candidates. Subsequent generations therefore compare submasks or intersections of earlier masks, not independent K sparse starts. This also makes elimination ambiguous: it is unclear whether the algorithm discards a binary mask, a masked weight matrix, or a candidate with its own training history. The paper later acknowledges that the chosen fitness can make elimination effectively pointless, which further undercuts the evolutionary-search interpretation and claims about the role of K unless mask-switching or elimination effects are measured.

comments.title: Magnitude fitness appears to use raw signed sums rather than weight magnitudes
comments.quote: W ← individual with maximum sum (of weights);
comments.explanation: The text calls the criterion “magnitude,” but the equations and algorithm repeatedly describe selecting the individual with the maximum raw sum of weights. For neural-network weights with mixed signs, a raw signed sum is not a magnitude or importance measure: positive and negative weights cancel, large negative weights may be penalized despite being important, and masks can be favored merely because they retain more positive initialized weights. Standard magnitude pruning uses absolute values, squared values, or related norms. If the intended criterion is sum of absolute weights, the paper should state this consistently; if it is truly the signed sum, it needs justification for why signed total weight is a meaningful fitness function.

comments.title: Dissipating-gradients threshold is inconsistent across text and algorithm
comments.quote: In equation 4 epsilon is 1e-6 for all experiments.
comments.explanation: The text specifies epsilon as 1e-6, but Algorithm 2 applies the condition `if accumulated d W < 0.0001`, which corresponds to 1e-4. This difference can materially change how many weights are pruned, especially early in training when accumulated gradients may be small. The paper does not make clear which threshold was actually used in the experiments, so the equation, algorithm, and experimental description should be reconciled.

comments.title: Dissipating-gradients criterion uses signed accumulated gradients and may prune actively updated weights
comments.explanation: The method is motivated as removing weights that are not being updated, but the written algorithm thresholds the signed accumulated gradient, not its magnitude. A weight can receive substantial updates whose signed gradients cancel across mini-batches, or it can have a large negative accumulated gradient, and still satisfy `accumulated dW < 0.0001`. Small summed gradients also do not necessarily imply unimportance; they can arise from cancellation, saturation, optimizer dynamics, or local flatness. This is especially relevant because the paper uses Adam, whose parameter updates are not simply raw summed gradients. If the intended rule is elementwise `|accumulated dW| < epsilon` or accumulated absolute gradients, that should be made explicit.

comments.title: Accumulated-gradient state is not clearly initialized or reset between pruning epochs
comments.explanation: The pseudocode does not specify how `accumulated dW` is initialized, whether it is reset at the start of each epoch, or whether the binary pruning mask is stored separately from the gradient accumulator. As written, after one epoch `accumulated dW` is overwritten by 0/1 values; in the next epoch, `accumulated dW ← accumulated dW + dW` would mix a binary mask with raw gradients. Different interpretations would produce different sparsity patterns and performance, so this state handling must be specified for reproducibility.

comments.title: Claims of consistent superiority for combination dropout are stronger than the evidence
comments.quote: We provide a combination dropout approach that consistently outperforms other dropout approaches.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that the combination approach performs better than either component method and consistently outperforms other dropout approaches. However, the reported evidence is limited to MNIST and Fashion-MNIST with small fully connected or autoencoder architectures, and performance depends on choices such as p, K, threshold, and fitness function. Without broader architectures, datasets, hyperparameter sweeps, or statistical testing, the claim should be restricted to the reported experimental settings rather than stated categorically.

comments.title: Experimental setup is underspecified for reproducing the comparisons
comments.quote: The experiments performed on two datasets; MNIST Deng (2012) and Fashion MNIST Xiao et al. (2017). The network architectures are two layered Autoencoder (784-128-64), a three layered NN (784-128-100-10) both with sigmoid activation and adam optimization functions.
comments.explanation: The paper gives the datasets and broad architectures, but key reproducibility details are missing: Adam learning rate and optimizer parameters, batch size, loss functions, number of epochs or iterations, train/test or validation protocol, values of K and p, elimination frequency, dissipating-gradient threshold, and how target sparsity is computed after combining methods. The autoencoder architecture is also ambiguous because `784-128-64` describes an encoder bottleneck but not the decoder or reconstruction output. Because pruning results are sensitive to these choices, the described setup is insufficient to reproduce the reported curves or compare methods fairly.

comments.title: Alternative gradient-based fitness criteria are underspecified and cancellation-prone
comments.quote:
- **Gradient:** The second choice is to use gradient of the weights to create population as follows:

- _population_ = _δW ∗ SparseMatrices_ (5)
comments.explanation: The paper discusses gradient-based and summed-gradient alternatives, but does not specify the actual scoring formula, accumulation window, whether gradients are taken before or after Adam transformations, or whether absolute values/norms are used. The displayed formula uses raw signed gradients multiplied by masks, and the surrounding text suggests signed accumulation. Raw signed gradients are not direct importance measures: large negative gradients may be just as salient as positive ones, and signs can cancel across parameters or iterations. A reader cannot tell whether the intended score is a signed sum, sum of absolute gradients, norm, or loss-change approximation.

comments.title: Experimental interpretations about architecture, K, and p overstate what the reported ablations show
comments.quote: We initially use single layer NN (784-10) to isolate the effects of kstarts algorithm from effects of other parameters that may have a large impact on performance as the size of the network grows.
comments.explanation: The single-layer experiments are useful sanity checks, but a 784-10 model is essentially a linear classifier, so sparsity there does not fully represent sparsity in hidden layers of multilayer networks. Similarly, the explanation that lower K performs better because larger K causes the network to switch masks more often is not supported without reporting mask-switch counts or selected-mask trajectories. The table also appears to compare K values at a fixed p rather than showing a general relationship among K, p, and iteration count. These results can support limited empirical observations, but the causal and architectural conclusions should be stated more cautiously.

comments.title: Dropout is conflated with pruning/sparsity-producing methods
comments.quote: Post-training dropout based approaches achieve high sparsity and are well established means of deciphering problems relating to computational cost and overfitting in Neural Network architectures citesrivastava2014dropout, (Pan et al., 2016), Zhu & Gupta (2017), LeCun et al. (1990).
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a broad motivation sentence, so I checked whether the later context makes clear that the authors are using “dropout” loosely to mean pruning. The paper indeed repeatedly compares its methods to “random dropout” and cites both pruning papers and dropout-style regularization papers together. What remains problematic is that standard dropout in the sense of Srivastava et al. (2014) does not by itself produce a sparse final network or reduce inference-time computational cost; units are randomly dropped during training and the full dense network is normally used at test time.
