comments.title: Broad claims about initialization pruning are not supported by the paper’s scope
comments.quote: Initialization pruning is more efficacious when it comes to scaling computation cost of the network. Furthermore, it handles overfitting just as well as post training dropout. It is also averse to retraining losses.
comments.explanation: I treated this as abstract-level positioning and checked the later experimental context. The experiments are on MNIST/Fashion-MNIST with fully connected networks and autoencoders, and the limitations section explicitly notes that sparse-matrix implementations and time comparisons are not used. Thus the paper may be able to claim that the proposed methods preserve accuracy on these small settings, but the wording here is much stronger: it asserts general superiority for computation scaling and comparable overfitting control to post-training dropout. Standard practice would require either runtime/operation-count evidence under an actual sparse implementation or a clearly qualified theoretical argument. As written, the claim overstates what is demonstrated in the provided context.

comments.title: Dissipating gradients is described as pruning by distance from initialization, but later algorithm prunes by accumulated gradient
comments.quote: 2) Dissipating gradients approach, aims at eliminating weights that remain within a fraction of their initial value during the first couple of epochs.
comments.explanation: At first this sounded like a reasonable heuristic: prune weights whose values have barely moved from initialization. I checked the later description and Algorithm 2 in the full context, however, and the implemented rule appears to accumulate gradients over iterations and zero weights whose accumulated gradient is below a fixed threshold. Those are not equivalent criteria. A weight can remain close to its initial value because signed gradients cancel, because the learning rate is small, or because it is already near a useful value; conversely, a small accumulated gradient threshold does not directly measure a fraction of the initial weight value. This mismatch matters because the paper’s intuition and reproducibility depend on knowing whether the pruning statistic is displacement from initialization, absolute/signed accumulated gradient, or something else.

comments.title: The central K-starts operation is underspecified: ordinary product, Hadamard mask, or set construction
comments.quote: _population_ = _W ∗ K − SparseMatrices_
comments.explanation: The prose helps: the authors appear to mean applying each of K binary sparsity masks to the current weight matrix. I therefore assume the intended operation is an elementwise/Hadamard product producing K masked weight matrices, not a conventional matrix product. Still, the formal notation is ambiguous because W and each sparse matrix have the same shape, so an ordinary matrix product would generally be dimensionally invalid, while “K − SparseMatrices” could be read as a scalar/matrix expression rather than a set of masks. For a pruning algorithm, the exact masking operation is central: whether the mask is binary, whether it is sampled once or resampled, whether it is per layer, and whether gradients flow through masked entries all affect reproducibility. The surrounding text partially resolves intent but not enough for an implementer to reproduce the method unambiguously.

comments.title: The term “individual” alternates between binary mask and masked weight matrix
comments.quote: The basic components of the algorithm are: (1) **Population** : A product of network weights and sparse matrices. (2) **Individual** : An instance of the population.
comments.explanation: This definition says an individual is an instance of the population, i.e. a masked weight matrix W multiplied by one sparse mask. But the next subsection describes each individual as a sparse matrix with 0’s and 1’s determined by p. Those are different objects: a binary mask versus the masked weights obtained after applying that mask. I checked whether the later algorithm removes the ambiguity, but it also uses phrases such as passing an individual as W, which reinforces the confusion. This is not merely terminology: fitness, gradient updates, elimination, and inheritance behave differently depending on whether the candidate object is the mask or the actual masked weight tensor.

comments.title: Fitness based on a raw sum of weights is ambiguous and may be mathematically inappropriate
comments.quote: The fitness of an individual is ranked by determining the sum of each individual in population as given in 1 such that the fittest individual in a generation is given by equation 2.
comments.explanation: The abstract says fitness can be based on the “magnitude” of weights, so I considered whether “sum” here might be shorthand for an L1 norm or sum of absolute values. But the text does not state absolute value, and the later algorithm in the full context also says “maximum sum (of weights).” If the raw signed sum is used, positive and negative weights cancel, and a mask can be favored merely because it retains more positive-valued initialized weights, not because it preserves important connections or improves loss. Neural-network weights are commonly initialized around zero, so a signed-sum criterion is especially unstable and sign-biased. If the intended criterion is sum of magnitudes, that should be explicit; if the intended criterion is raw sum, the paper needs justification for why that is a meaningful fitness function for pruning.

comments.title: Passing the fittest masked weights as W can unintentionally make masks intersect over time
comments.quote: In every iteration we multiply model weights W of the Network layer in question with every instance of the K sparse matrices. The resulting set of matrices is our population for that iteration. Each iteration is referred to as a new generation.
comments.explanation: The intended analogy seems to be that K candidate sparse subnetworks compete and the best candidate is retained. I then checked the next-generation description: “The fittest individual is passed off as weight to the next generation.” If W is replaced by one masked version W ⊙ M_j, and the next generation again forms W ⊙ M_k for all masks, then any weight zeroed by the selected mask remains zero for all future candidates. Subsequent selections effectively take intersections of masks rather than continuing to evaluate the original K p-sparse subnetworks. That behavior can rapidly increase sparsity and makes elimination of low-fitness masks conceptually different from choosing among fixed lottery-ticket candidates. If this destructive masking is intended, it needs to be stated and analyzed; if it is not intended, the update rule should preserve a dense master weight matrix or maintain separate candidate weights.

comments.title: Eliminating candidate masks is not well defined once W has already been overwritten by the fittest individual
comments.quote:
- The fittest individual is passed of as weight to the next generation.

- Every 5 generations or as per the decided elimination frequency, the individual with lowest fitness is discarded from the population.
comments.explanation: I initially interpreted this as an evolutionary selection step, which is a plausible high-level idea. But given the earlier definition that the population is regenerated each iteration by multiplying the current W with each sparse matrix, it is unclear what exactly is discarded: a binary mask, a masked weight matrix from the current generation, or a separate candidate with its own training history. If W has already been overwritten by the fittest masked weights, discarding another current masked individual does not by itself preserve or remove a trained model; it only possibly removes one mask from future consideration. This makes the evolutionary analogy and the algorithmic effect of the elimination frequency hard to reproduce. The later design discussion even suggests elimination may be pointless under the magnitude criterion, which reinforces that the selection dynamics are underspecified here.

comments.title: Claimed faster computation is not established by the described method
comments.quote: This provides an edge for faster computation, quicker elimination of excess weights and reduced generalization error.
comments.explanation: The statement is plausible in principle: a fixed sparse network can be cheaper than a dense one if sparse operations are actually exploited. I checked the full context, though, and the limitations section says sparse-matrix algorithms are not used and no time comparison is shown. In addition, K-starts initially evaluates K masked versions of the weights, which may increase computation unless implemented carefully. Therefore the paper can reasonably claim a reduction in active parameters, but not yet an empirical or practical speed advantage. The distinction matters because sparse neural networks often do not yield wall-clock speedups without hardware/software support and sufficiently structured sparsity.

comments.title: Threshold for dissipating gradients is inconsistent across text and algorithm
comments.quote: In equation 4 epsilon is 1e-6 for all experiments.
comments.explanation: I first read this as specifying the pruning threshold for the dissipating-gradients mask. Later, however, Algorithm 2 applies the criterion `if accumulated d W <  0 . 0001`, i.e. a threshold of 1e-4 rather than 1e-6. This does not look like a harmless formatting issue: 1e-4 versus 1e-6 can materially change how many weights are pruned, especially when accumulated gradients are small in early epochs. The surrounding context does not resolve which value was actually used in the experiments. If equation 4 really defines epsilon as 1e-6, Algorithm 2 and the experimental description need to be reconciled.

comments.title: Dissipating-gradients criterion uses signed accumulated gradients, which may prune actively updated weights
comments.explanation: The authors' intent seems to be to remove weights that are not being updated, which is a plausible early-pruning heuristic. But the algorithm appears to threshold the signed accumulated gradient, not its magnitude or an accumulated absolute gradient. A weight can receive substantial updates whose signed gradients cancel across mini-batches, or it can have a large negative accumulated gradient, and under the written rule it would satisfy `accumulated d W < 0.0001` and be pruned. That does not match the prose claim that the method eliminates weights whose weights are not getting updated. Standard gradient-saliency pruning methods usually use magnitudes, absolute accumulated gradients, or quantities such as |w ⋅ ∂L/∂w| precisely to avoid sign-cancellation artifacts. If the intended condition is elementwise `|accumulated dW| < epsilon`, that should be stated explicitly.

comments.title: Accumulated-gradient state is not clearly initialized or reset between pruning epochs
comments.explanation: I can infer that `accumulated dW` is intended to become a binary pruning mask after each epoch. However, the pseudocode does not say how it is initialized, whether the gradient accumulator is reset at the start of each epoch, or whether the binary mask is kept separately from the next epoch's gradient accumulation. As written, after one epoch `accumulated dW` is overwritten by 0/1 values; in the next epoch the line `accumulated d W ← accumulated d W + dW` mixes a binary mask with raw gradients. That would make the later pruning criterion depend on a previous mask offset rather than only on accumulated gradients. This is not just a presentation detail, because different interpretations would produce different sparsity patterns and performance.

comments.title: K-starts weight replacement may collapse the population rather than compare independent sparse starts
comments.quote: Every time the weight W needs to be updated instead of the usual gradient update we add one step further and using the fitness function, pass the fittest individual as the new W which biases the network towards that sparse matrix.
comments.explanation: The high-level idea of K-starts is clear: evaluate several random sparse masks and keep the fittest one. But the algorithm says to replace the actual weight matrix `W` by the selected masked individual after each update. Since each individual is defined as `W` multiplied by a sparse mask, setting `W` to one masked individual makes all weights zeroed by that chosen mask unavailable to later candidates. In the next generation, multiplying this already-masked `W` by other masks compares submasks of the previously selected mask, not the original K independent sparse starts. The later design discussion acknowledges that the magnitude-based choice skews selection toward the previously selected matrix, but the algorithm still presents this as an evolutionary K-starts search. This affects the core methodology: the population may effectively collapse after the first selected mask, making the elimination schedule and K less meaningful than claimed.

comments.title: Fitness based on raw sum of weights is not a reliable magnitude or importance criterion
comments.quote: W ← individual with maximum sum (of weights);
comments.explanation: I checked whether the surrounding text resolves this by saying the fitness is based on magnitude, gradients, or both. However, the algorithm itself uses the maximum raw sum of weights, and the elimination step uses the minimum raw sum. For neural-network weights initialized with mixed signs, the raw sum is highly sensitive to sign cancellations and arbitrary sign conventions: a mask containing many large negative but important weights could have a low sum, while a mask with small positive weights could score higher. That is different from magnitude-based pruning, which normally uses absolute values or squared magnitudes. If the intended operation is the sum of absolute weights, the algorithm should say so; if it is truly the signed sum, the paper needs to justify why signed total weight is a meaningful fitness measure.

comments.title: Combination-dropout performance claim is stronger than the evidence stated at this point
comments.quote: The approach achieves better performance than either methods.
comments.explanation: The intended claim is understandable: combining a fixed K-starts mask with additional dissipating-gradient pruning may exploit complementary signals. But the statement is categorical, and the available context later evaluates only MNIST and Fashion-MNIST with small fully connected networks and autoencoders. No statistical test, hyperparameter sweep, or architectural breadth is provided in this passage to justify a general claim that the combination achieves better performance than either method. A safer claim would be that it performed better in the reported experiments, or under the tested settings. As written, it risks overstating what has been demonstrated.

comments.title: Experimental setup is underspecified for reproducing the reported comparisons
comments.quote: The experiments performed on two datasets; MNIST Deng (2012) and Fashion MNIST Xiao et al. (2017). The network architectures are two layered Autoencoder (784-128-64), a three layered NN (784-128-100-10) both with sigmoid activation and adam optimization functions.
comments.explanation: This gives the datasets and broad architectures, which is a useful start. But for reproducing pruning comparisons, several key details are missing: Adam learning rate and other optimizer parameters, batch size, loss functions, number of epochs or iterations for each figure, train/test split or validation protocol, values of K, p, elimination frequency, dissipating-gradient threshold, and how target sparsity is computed after combining methods. The autoencoder architecture is also ambiguous: `784-128-64` describes an encoder bottleneck but not the decoder/output reconstruction layer. Because pruning results can be sensitive to these choices, the method section as written is not sufficient for a careful reader to reproduce the reported curves or compare the methods fairly.

comments.title: Dissipating-gradients description conflates small gradients with lack of useful weight updates
comments.quote: Based upon that hypothesis, in each epoch we sum gradients over and eliminate weights whose weights are not getting updated.
comments.explanation: The intuition is reasonable: early in training, weights receiving little gradient signal may be less useful. However, the text treats small accumulated gradients as equivalent to weights not being updated or not influencing results. In stochastic neural-network training, small summed gradients can arise from cancellation across examples, saturation of sigmoid units, optimizer dynamics, or local flatness, and these do not necessarily imply that a weight is unimportant. This concern is partly acknowledged by the later warning not to apply the method for too many epochs, but the pruning rule itself still needs more justification or empirical validation, especially because the paper uses Adam, whose actual parameter updates are not simply the raw summed gradients.

comments.title: Magnitude fitness appears to use signed sums rather than magnitudes
comments.quote: - **Magnitude:** As opted here, we choose population as shown in equation 1 and then select fitness using equation 2.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intent: they are describing the actual fitness criterion used in their k-starts experiments and contrasting it with gradient-based alternatives. However, from the surrounding algorithmic context, equation 2 and Algorithm 1 appear to select the individual with the maximum sum of weights, not the maximum sum of absolute weights or another magnitude norm. That is a substantive distinction. In standard pruning language, “magnitude” normally means absolute value, e.g. \(|W_{ij}|\), because signed weights can cancel and because a large negative weight can be just as important as a large positive weight. If the fitness is really the raw signed sum of masked weights, then the method preferentially selects masks covering positive-valued weights under the current initialization/training state, rather than masks containing high-magnitude or high-saliency weights. If the intended equation used absolute values, the text should say so consistently; this is not just an OCR-level symbol issue because the prose repeatedly says “sum” and Algorithm 1 says “maximum sum (of weights).”

comments.title: The chosen k-starts fitness is acknowledged to make elimination pointless, but later claims still rely on K and elimination
comments.quote: This also renders elimination to be pointless it does not matter if 1 or all other matrices in K are eliminated. Furthermore, the sparse matrix is picked awfully early in the experiments i.e. only after first 5 iterations or so and that is not when weights have reached a saturation point.
comments.explanation: The authors deserve credit for candidly identifying a weakness of their chosen magnitude-based fitness criterion. But after checking the surrounding sections, this creates a deeper unresolved methodological problem. The paper elsewhere frames k-starts as an evolutionary-style selection process in which the population size K and periodic elimination help identify a good sparse subnetwork. Here the authors state that, under the fitness function actually used, eliminating individuals is effectively pointless and the selected sparse matrix is fixed extremely early. That seems to undercut the interpretation of k-starts as an evolutionary search over masks, and it also weakens later discussion of the “effect of K.” If elimination does not matter for the implemented criterion, then results attributed to K-starts should be reinterpreted as early random-mask selection with a particular signed-weight heuristic, unless the authors provide evidence that K or elimination still changes the selected mask in practice.

comments.title: Gradient-based fitness is underspecified and appears to ignore gradient sign conventions
comments.quote:
- **Gradient:** The second choice is to use gradient of the weights to create population as follows:

- _population_ = _δW ∗ SparseMatrices_ (5)

- doing so can have fitness totally dependant on the current update and the new weights are heavily skewed towards the performance of the current iterations which again doesn’t seem that appropriate.
comments.explanation: I see that this is presented as an alternative design choice rather than necessarily the final method. Still, the description is technically ambiguous in a way that matters. Earlier context says fitness may be based on gradient magnitude, but the displayed formula uses raw \(\delta W\) multiplied by masks, and the selection rule elsewhere is a maximum sum. In gradient descent, the sign of a gradient is not a direct measure of importance: a large negative gradient may be just as salient as a large positive one, and signed gradients can cancel across parameters. Standard gradient-based saliency criteria usually use absolute values, squared gradients, norms, or loss-change approximations rather than raw signed sums. A reader cannot tell whether the intended fitness is \(\sum M\odot \delta W\), \(\sum |M\odot \delta W|\), a norm, or something evaluated through the training loss. That ambiguity affects reproducibility and the interpretation of why this option was rejected.

comments.title: Sum-of-gradients fitness lacks a concrete formula and would be sensitive to cancellation
comments.quote:
- **Sum of Gradients:** The third option is summing up the gradients for a number of iterations in the manner we do for dissipating gradients 3 and then use those to create population:

Doing so skews the network toward weights that are updated quickly and are increasing.
comments.explanation: The intended idea is understandable: accumulated gradients might be more stable than a single minibatch gradient. However, the method is not specified enough for a reader to reproduce it. The text says to sum gradients “in the manner we do for dissipating gradients,” but does not give the actual population formula, the accumulation window, whether gradients are accumulated before or after optimizer transformations such as Adam moments, whether absolute gradients are used, or how the resulting population is scored. The phrase “weights that are updated quickly and are increasing” suggests signed accumulation, which would systematically favor positive updates and may cancel weights with alternating or negative gradients even when they are important. This is a technical gap because the paper elsewhere treats gradient accumulation as a plausible pruning criterion.

comments.title: Single-layer experiments do not fully justify the claim that the method works independently of architectural factors
comments.quote: We initially use single layer NN (784-10) to isolate the effects of kstarts algorithm from effects of other parameters that may have a large impact on performance as the size of the network grows. Those parameters i.e. number of hidden layers, regularization choices, types of layers may aid or adversely effect the performance of the algorithm and by performing the experiments on the basic unit of a neural network we were able to concur that the method is effective in it’s own right.
comments.explanation: I understand the experimental motivation: using a minimal architecture can reduce confounding from depth, hidden width, regularization, and layer type. But the conclusion is stronger than what this setup can establish. A 784-10 single-layer model on MNIST/Fashion-MNIST is essentially a linear classifier over pixels, so a sparse mask there behaves differently from sparsity in hidden layers of a multilayer network, where feature representations and inter-layer dependencies matter. Such an experiment can be a useful sanity check, but it does not by itself isolate all relevant factors or show that k-starts is effective “in its own right” in the broader neural-network setting. The later tests on a three-layer NN and autoencoder help, but the reasoning here still overstates what the single-layer ablation demonstrates.

comments.title: Cost-effectiveness claim is not demonstrated by the experiments
comments.quote: One beneficial feature of pre-training pruning approaches is that the best p-sparse network is quickly identified. There are a number of methods that exploit sparsity of a matrix for quicker multiplication i.e. (Yuster & Zwick, 2005), (Buluc¸ & Gilbert, 2012) which can be used to quickly retrain large networks. Although that is out of scope for our findings.
comments.explanation: The authors are right that sparse matrix algorithms can, in principle, reduce computation when sparsity is represented and exploited efficiently. However, the passage makes two claims that are not established here. First, saying that “the best p-sparse network is quickly identified” is stronger than the evidence supports; the method selects a mask by a heuristic fitness function, not by an exhaustive or validated search over sparse subnetworks. Second, the computational benefit is only potential unless the implementation actually uses sparse kernels and reports wall-clock, FLOP, memory, or throughput comparisons. The limitations section later acknowledges that such algorithms were not used, so this cost-effectiveness discussion should be framed as a possible future benefit rather than a demonstrated feature of the proposed methods.

comments.title: Proposed explanation for lower K is not supported by the described algorithm or measurements
comments.quote: From the experiments done so far lower K ( _≈_ 10) outperforms or at least performs as well as a higher value of K ( _≈_ 100). This can be because the more times a different matrix is chosen by the network, the more times the network has to adopt to learning with only that p-sparse network.
comments.explanation: The empirical observation is plausible and is broadly consistent with parts of Table 1. The concern is with the causal explanation. The algorithm does not show that a larger K necessarily causes the selected mask to change more often; depending on the fitness rule, a single mask may dominate early and persist. Earlier in this same section, the authors even state that the magnitude criterion tends to propagate the initially selected sparse matrix and makes elimination pointless, which would work against the proposed explanation. To support this interpretation, the paper would need to report mask-switch counts, selected-mask trajectories, or some measure of instability as K increases. As written, this is a reasonable hypothesis but is presented more like an explanation of the observed result.

comments.title: Table 1 does not clearly support the stated relationship among k, p, and iterations
comments.quote:
Table 1 shows relationship between k,p and no of iterations in a single layer NN (784-10)

Table 1: MNIST-Mean Accuracy and standard deviation averaged over 10 runs

|||**p=0.0**||**p=0.5**|**p=0.5**||
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
||||k=1|k=10|k=50|k=100|
comments.explanation: I considered whether this is partly an OCR or multicolumn-table extraction issue, and the duplicated \(p=0.5\) header may indeed reflect a formatting artifact. Even under the most plausible intended reading, however, the table seems to compare a fully connected baseline \(p=0.0\) with several K values at \(p=0.5\), rather than varying \(p\) over multiple sparsity levels. That makes it hard to see how the table shows a general relationship between \(k\), \(p\), and iteration count, or how it supports the nearby claim that an appropriate value such as \(p\approx 0.5\) works best. It can support claims about K at a fixed sparsity, but the p-dependence needs either more p values in the table or a clearer pointer to the figure that varies sparsity.

comments.title: Conclusion overstates consistency of combination dropout across settings
comments.quote: We provide a combination dropout approach that consistently outperforms other dropout approaches.
comments.explanation: The authors do qualify in adjacent sentences that more analysis is needed across datasets, tasks, and architectures, which helps. Still, the word “consistently” is stronger than the evidence presented in this paper appears to warrant. The experiments are limited to MNIST and Fashion-MNIST with single-layer or small fully connected/autoencoder architectures, and the performance of k-starts and combination dropout is described elsewhere as depending on the chosen \(p\), K, and fitness function. A more defensible statement would restrict the consistency claim to the reported experimental settings and tested sparsity ranges. As written, a reader could reasonably take it as a broader claim about dropout/pruning methods in general.
